title: Chemical Composition Optimization for Austenitic Steels of the Fe-Mn-Al-C System
abstract_summary: This study systematically investigates composition limits in Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels to avoid embrittling phases (δ-ferrite, β-Mn, grain-boundary carbides) and to achieve structural properties. Effects of Al, C, and Mn on age-hardening, tensile strength, and impact toughness are analyzed. An optimum composition window (Al 9–9.5%, C 0.9–0.95%, Mn 28.5–30.5%) is proposed for UTS > 1100 MPa and impact toughness > 50 J/cm² after quenching and aging at 550 °C.
research_objective: To determine the best combination of composition ranges for basic alloying elements in Fe-Mn-Al-C steels to obtain an optimum microstructural state (absence of δ-ferrite, coarse grain-boundary precipitation, and β-Mn) and a combination of tensile strength ≥ 1100 MPa and impact toughness ≥ 50 J/cm² for structural applications.
main_process_variables: Alloy composition: Al (3–10 wt%), C (0.3–1.2 wt%), Mn (24–34 wt%)
Mechanical testing: tensile strength (UTS), U-notch impact toughness (K_CU)

key_sections.role: States the lack of systematic composition criteria and defines the objective of finding optimum composition windows for structural Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.
Describes preparation of 31 alloys with varying Al, C, Mn; thermomechanical processing; and tensile and impact testing.

key_sections.section: Results and Discussion: Effects of Aluminum Content
key_sections.role: Analyzes effect of Al on age-hardening, onset of κ-phase precipitation above ~7% Al, and its influence on strength and impact toughness.

key_sections.section: Results and Discussion: Effects of Carbon Content
key_sections.role: Shows effect of C on mechanical properties (Fig. 6) at 9% Al, 30% Mn after quenching and aging; highlights trade-off between strength and ductility/toughness.

key_sections.section: Results and Discussion: Effects of Manganese Content
key_sections.role: Demonstrates Mn must be 26–31% to avoid δ-ferrite and β-Mn; presents mechanical property trends vs Mn content after aging (Fig. 7).

key_sections.section: Results and Discussion: Optimization of the Steel Composition
key_sections.role: Uses second-order polynomial modeling of UTS and K_CU as functions of composition, yielding optimum ranges: Al 9–9.5%, C 0.9–0.95%, Mn 28.5–30.5% for target properties.

Summarizes microstructural composition limits (6.2 < Al < 9.7%, 0.7 < C < 1%, 25 < Mn < 31%) and narrower limits for combination of high strength and toughness (Al 9–9.5%, C 0.9–0.95%, Mn 28.5–30.5%).
